data.title: Trump administration reviews new contenders to lead Federal Reserve - report
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's team is expanding it search for the next Federal Reserve chief, adding former St. Louis Fed President James Bullard and economic consultant Mark Summerlin to a growing list of contenders, according to a media report published Friday.

It was reported Thursday that Fed Governor Christopher Waller is gaining momentum in the process to succeed Jerome Powell as chair of the central bank.

Former Fed governor Kevin Warsh and Kevin Hassett, director of the National Economic Council, are also still in the running for the role.

In all, Bullard and Summerlin have been added to a roughly 10-person roster of candidates being weighed for the Fed's top post, the Wall Street Journal reported, citing senior administration officials.

Trump is tapping Treasury Scott Bessent to lead chair search, the officials added. And he will handle the first round of candidate interviews before narrowing the field and passing a shortlist along to Trump for final meetings.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent had been considered as a candidate for the chair role, but Trump said he wanted to continue his current role.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Palantin Technologies to enact 1-for-50 reverse stock split
data.description:
• Palatin Technologies (OTC:PTNT) will implement a 1-for-50 reverse stock split effective August 8, 2025.
  • Trading on a split-adjusted basis begins August 11, 2025.
  • The split aims to raise the per-share price to meet NYSE American listing requirements.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Under Armour outlines $100M tariff headwind and plans SKU reduction to drive brand transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Under Armour (UAA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Kevin A. Plank described the company as being in a “bold reinvention and rebuilding with purpose to become a sharper, more focused brand one that blends sports, style and innovation with financial discipline and edge.” Plank emphasized, “we're undertaking a bold reinvention and rebuilding with purpose to become a sharper, more focused brand one that blends sports, style and innovation with financial discipline and edge.” He highlighted a shift to a brand-first strategy, consolidation of product lines, and a goal to reduce SKUs by 25%.

  • Plank announced, “we've already cut our materials by 30% for our 2025 products and plan to reduce it further in 2026, lowering costs, improving sourcing and supporting more sustainable innovation-driven design.” He also discussed a two-lever strategy: launching “pinnacle defining products” at higher price points and redesigning top volume items for better performance and higher ASPs.

  • The CEO stated, “we're shifting from a gym first approach to a focus on team sports, emphasizing both American and global football, basketball, baseball and volleyball,” and is integrating a women’s-centered approach into category management. Plank acknowledged footwear underperformance but outlined a reset with new leadership and a focus on the Velociti and Halo franchises.

  • CFO David E. Bergman commented, “we're pleased to have delivered another quarter that met or slightly exceeded our outlook on every line item. First quarter revenue declined 4% to $1.1 billion.” Bergman detailed regional results, highlighted SG&A savings, and noted, “our first quarter gross margin increased by 70 basis points year-over-year to 48.2%.”

Outlook

  • Management expects “approximately $100 million in additional tariff-related costs, along with softer-than-expected demand in fiscal '26.” Bergman said, “our profitability is projected to be about half of what it was last year.” The company is “actively reviewing our cost structure to find additional efficiencies.”

  • For Q2, Under Armour expects “revenue will decline 6% to 7% year-over-year,” with North America anticipated to see a “low double-digit decline” and APAC “down at a low teen rate.” EMEA is expected to deliver “high single-digit revenue growth.” Gross margin for Q2 is projected to decline by “340 to 360 basis points compared to last year.” Adjusted operating income for the quarter is expected between $30 million and $40 million, with adjusted diluted EPS at $0.01 to $0.02.

Financial Results

  • First quarter revenue declined 4% to $1.1 billion. North America revenue declined 5%, EMEA increased 10%, APAC decreased 10%, and Latin America declined 15%. Wholesale revenue declined 5%, DTC declined 3% with e-commerce down 12%, and owned store sales increased 1%. Licensing revenues grew 12%.

  • Apparel revenue declined 1%, footwear revenue was down 14%, and accessories grew 8%. Gross margin increased to 48.2%. SG&A expenses decreased 37% to $530 million, with adjusted SG&A at $522 million. Reported diluted loss per share was ($0.01), while adjusted diluted EPS was $0.02. Inventory at the end of Q1 was $1.1 billion, and the cash balance stood at $911 million.

Q&A

  • Jay Daniel Sole, UBS: Asked about tariff impacts on wholesale demand and green shoots in brand health. Plank responded that the tariff environment is “not ideal,” but the company’s two-lever strategy is “attacking tariffs,” and he sees factual improvement in brand health, citing “double-digit types of sell-throughs” and an NPS score that has increased “by 18 points year-over-year to nearly 70.”

  • Peter Clement McGoldrick, Stifel: Asked about SKU cleanup and promotional pullback effects on Q2. Bergman said, “Q2...is probably going to be our toughest quarter as we think through the year.” On marketing to younger demographics, Plank noted, “brand perception is up in the mid-single digits...18 to 34, the brand awareness is moving up.”

  • Samuel Marc Poser, Williams Trading: Asked about management tone, brand improvement translation, tax rate, and order book. Plank described “much calmer and clear confidence,” citing structural improvements and “metrics, as I've stated, some of the NPS scores...brand awareness and perception.” Bergman explained tax complexities and noted product momentum with retailers.

  • Brian William Nagel, Oppenheimer: Asked about course corrections and operational structure. Plank emphasized a focus on “selling so much more, so much less at a much higher full retail price” and stressed the foundation is in place for execution.

  • Paul Lawrence Lejuez, Citi: Inquired about Q2 North America sales decline and price increases. Bergman detailed order book softness, traffic challenges, and more headwinds in footwear. On pricing, he said targeted increases are in place, with broader pricing benefits expected in fiscal '27.

  • Brooke Siler Roach, Goldman Sachs: Questioned execution confidence in North America and DTC actions. Plank expressed confidence, stating, “there's real context of what we're seeing in some of the NPS scores...awareness and perception.” Bergman discussed e-commerce pressure from promotions and efforts to hold the line on discounting.

  • Laurent Andre Vasilescu, BNP Paribas: Asked about the North America outlook and gross margin guide. Bergman confirmed tariff costs are the largest driver of margin pressure, “the big factor is the year-over-year 200 basis point hit on the tariff costs.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a tone of concern over tariffs, demand, promotional pressures, and the trajectory of brand transformation, often seeking proof of improvement and specifics on execution. Repeated questions about the timeline for visible improvements and the impact of pricing strategies indicated skepticism about how soon results will show.

  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and responses, with Plank offering assurances such as, “we're better than we were a year ago, we were better than we were 6 months ago, and I have great confidence, all the confidence that will be better 6 months from today.” Bergman’s statements were methodical, focused on the mitigation of headwinds and cost controls.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment was more focused on macro and tariff uncertainty, while management's confidence was steady, though more circumspect in acknowledging the magnitude of external pressures and slower near-term improvements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from previously anticipating gross margin expansion and modest revenue contraction to now projecting a 6% to 7% Q2 revenue decline and gross margin compression driven by $100 million in new tariff-related costs and softer demand.

  • Strategic focus moved from building brand and operational discipline to more urgent cost mitigation and aggressive product and SKU rationalization.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted from product innovation and marketing strategy to granular questions about tariff impacts, operational execution, and proof of early brand health improvements.

  • Key metrics such as gross margin and operating income are now forecasted to be significantly lower than the previous quarter’s expectations, reflecting the steeper macro and trade headwinds.

  • Management’s confidence remains, but with more measured language around timing, challenges, and the need for patience as the transformation takes hold.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified $100 million in incremental tariff-related costs and weaker demand as significant headwinds for fiscal '26, projecting operating income at roughly half of last year’s levels.

  • The outlook for North America and APAC is pressured by persistent softness in wholesale, DTC, and e-commerce, along with increased promotional activity and competitive intensity.

  • Analysts raised repeated concerns about the speed and effectiveness of brand transformation, the translation of brand metrics into sales, and the risk that cost pressures and pricing actions could further dampen demand.

  • Management is pursuing mitigation strategies, including cost sharing with suppliers, alternative sourcing, selective pricing, and SG&A discipline, but acknowledged most margin offsets will be realized beyond fiscal '26.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that Under Armour’s transformation remains on track, with foundational changes in product focus, operational discipline, and brand strategy underway. However, the current fiscal year will be marked by significant headwinds from tariffs and softer demand, resulting in compressed profitability and a measured pace of improvement. The leadership team maintains confidence in the long-term trajectory, driven by sharper execution, cost discipline, and renewed brand focus, while cautioning that the full financial benefits will require patience to materialize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ANI Pharmaceuticals raises 2025 guidance to $843M revenue and $7.35 EPS as Cortrophin Gel accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ANI Pharmaceuticals (ANIP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Nikhil Lalwani described Q2 2025 as a "record-setting quarter for our company with all-time overall company highs in net revenue, adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA and adjusted non-GAAP EPS, reflecting very strong momentum across both our Rare Disease and Generics business units." Lalwani highlighted accelerated demand for Cortrophin Gel, record new patient starts, positive retina franchise developments, and solid generics performance. The company raised 2025 guidance for total net revenues to $818 million to $843 million, adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA to $213 million to $223 million, and adjusted non-GAAP EPS to $6.98 to $7.35.
  • Lalwani reported, "Cortrophin Gel had an exceptional quarter with revenues of $81.6 million, up 66% year-over-year and 54% from the first quarter of 2025." He added that the launch of the prefilled syringe and a larger sales team contributed to the strong growth.
  • Lalwani stated, "We expect Rare Disease to account for approximately 57% of total company net revenues in the second half of 2025."
  • The ACTH market is projected to grow 36% to $933 million in 2025, and Lalwani noted, "the current number of patients on ACTH therapy remains significantly below historical levels, offering substantial room for expansion."
  • CFO Stephen Carey confirmed, "Total net revenues were $211.4 million, representing year-over-year growth of 53% on an as-reported basis and 37% on an organic basis, driven by strong growth for our lead rare disease asset, Cortrophin Gel and our Generics business. Adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA was $54.1 million and adjusted non-GAAP EPS was $1.80."
  • Carey further stated, "Non-GAAP gross margin was 64.9%, an increase of over 6 points from the prior year period, principally due to favorable mix towards Rare Disease products and strength in Generics driven by the prucalopride 180-day exclusivity."

Outlook

  • The company raised full-year 2025 net revenue guidance to $818 million to $843 million, up from the prior range of $768 million to $793 million.
  • Cortrophin Gel full-year revenue guidance increased to $322 million to $329 million, up from $265 million to $274 million, reflecting 63% to 66% year-over-year growth.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA is projected at $213 million to $223 million, increased from $195 million to $205 million.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP EPS is now expected between $6.98 and $7.35, up from $6.27 to $6.62.
  • The company expects generics revenue growth in the mid-teens but forecasts lower revenue in the second half due to prucalopride competition.
  • Retina franchise guidance was reduced to $87 million to $93 million from $97 million to $103 million due to persistent Medicare market access challenges.

Financial Results

  • Rare Disease and brands revenues reached $117.2 million in Q2 2025, approximately double the prior year period on an as-reported basis, with rare disease revenues at $104 million, up 111% from the prior year.
  • Cortrophin Gel revenues were $81.6 million, up 66% year-over-year and 54% sequentially, driven by increased volume and new patient starts.
  • ILUVIEN and YUTIQ revenues were $22.3 million, up from $16.1 million in the previous quarter.
  • Generics revenues were $90.3 million, a 22% increase over the prior year, with new launches and prucalopride exclusivity contributing to results.
  • Adjusted non-GAAP diluted earnings per share was $1.80 for Q2, up from $1.02 in the prior year period. Adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA was $54.1 million compared to $33.2 million in the prior year.
  • The company ended Q2 with $217.8 million in unrestricted cash, up from $149.8 million in Q1. Cash flow from operations in the first half was $110.8 million.

Q&A

  • Gary Jay Nachman, Raymond James: Questioned if Cortrophin's Q2 strength was due to seasonality or onetime factors, and asked about further sales force expansion and upside in ophthalmology and gout. CEO Lalwani responded that growth was "driven by the underlying demand" and not seasonality or onetime benefits. He said, "We do not contemplate adding at this time additional sales team members" but will continue to evaluate high ROI initiatives. Regarding expansion, "there is a multiyear strong growth runway for Cortrophin." Ophthalmology and gout were confirmed as drivers, with "substantial room for expansion."
  • Nachman followed up on capital allocation and gross margin trajectory. Lalwani said BD focus remains on rare disease expansion, and CFO Carey explained, "At the moment, our near to midterm goal for cash is to continue to accrue cash to the balance sheet... As we continue to generate cash, we can think about debt paydowns in the future." Carey reiterated gross margin guidance of 63% to 64% for the year, noting prucalopride benefit will not recur in the second half.
  • Faisal Ali Khurshid, Leerink Partners: Asked about drivers of ACTH class inflection, and prefilled syringe economics. Lalwani cited sales force expansion, acceleration in new indications and prefilled syringe adoption, and increased new prescribers. He confirmed "70% of our new enrollments have been with the prefilled syringe presentation." On economics, "there's a modest upward pricing advantage on...the prefilled syringe."
  • Daniel Kyle Krizay, Guggenheim Securities: Asked about specialties adopting the prefilled syringe and impact of NEW DAY trial results. Lalwani said adoption "has ramped across indications" and feedback on NEW DAY was positive, but "there isn't a specific upside that has been factored in to the back half of the year."
  • David A. Amsellem, Piper Sandler: Asked about payer landscape and future indications for Cortrophin. Lalwani said, "we brought competition to this category in 2022" and engagement with payers remains collaborative. Expansion into new areas is possible, but focus remains on current specialties with significant opportunity.
  • Ekaterina V. Knyazkova, JPMorgan: Asked about ACTH category potential and physician perception. Lalwani believes the market "can go well past the previous peak" with "substantial room for expansion." Chris Mutz added that the gout indication helps bring in new rheumatologists.
  • Brandon Richard Folkes, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about Cortrophin market share and R&D spend. Lalwani said market share is not the primary focus; growth is driven by getting Cortrophin to appropriate patients. Carey noted R&D spend can be lumpy due to timing of spend and highlighted ongoing investment in rare disease and generics.
  • Leszek Sulewski, Truist Securities: Asked about drivers of guidance increase and generics outlook. Lalwani attributed the Cortrophin guidance raise to faster sales force impact, acceleration in new indications, prefilled syringe uptake, and new prescriber addition. Generics R&D execution remains strong, with no material FDA delays.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts had a positive tone, frequently congratulating management on results and seeking details on sustainability and drivers of growth. There was some skepticism regarding whether Cortrophin's growth was one-off or sustainable, and questions on the ability to maintain gross margins and expand the retina business given market access headwinds.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and responses, using phrases such as "we remain confident," "we are delighted with the growing recognition," and "we look forward to delivering strong multiyear growth." In the Q&A, management directly addressed most analyst concerns and acknowledged challenges, especially related to Medicare access for retina products.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive in raising guidance and emphasizing momentum, while analysts appeared more interested in understanding the drivers behind the upgraded outlook and the sustainability of growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 was raised across all key metrics: net revenues, adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA, adjusted non-GAAP EPS, and Cortrophin Gel revenues.
  • Cortrophin Gel guidance increased to $322 million to $329 million from $265 million to $274 million, reflecting much higher growth expectations after a strong Q2.
  • Retina franchise guidance was revised downward due to ongoing Medicare market access challenges, whereas in Q1, management maintained prior guidance and expected improvement.
  • Generics segment growth remained solid, but the outlook for the second half is more cautious due to loss of prucalopride exclusivity.
  • Management's tone shifted to greater confidence in rare disease momentum and more caution around retina access issues, while analysts' questions shifted to focus on sustainability and market expansion rather than turnover and integration issues seen in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing Medicare market access challenges impacting ILUVIEN and YUTIQ, with guidance reduced to reflect no meaningful change in co-pay funding gaps for the remainder of the year.
  • Competitive entrants into the prucalopride market expected to reduce generics revenue in the second half.
  • The company lost a jury verdict in litigation with CG Oncology but intends to challenge the verdict through post-trial motions or appeal.
  • Management noted continued investment in supply chain resilience and indicated strong U.S.-based manufacturing as a mitigant to evolving tariff risks.

Final Takeaway

ANI Pharmaceuticals delivered a record second quarter with broad-based strength in Rare Disease and Generics, highlighted by exceptional demand for Cortrophin Gel and successful commercial execution. The company raised full-year guidance across all major metrics, supported by strong first-half results and accelerating momentum in key franchises, while acknowledging and adjusting for continued challenges in the retina market due to Medicare patient access. Management expressed confidence in sustaining multiyear growth trajectories, particularly for Cortrophin Gel, and remains committed to investing in both organic and business development-driven expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goodyear signals persistent market headwinds and delays in margin targets amid global trade disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company (GT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Mark W. Stewart, CEO and President, began by addressing that "our second quarter results were below our expectations and reflect an unprecedented level of industry disruption given changes in global trade that negatively impacted our consumer and commercial businesses globally." He emphasized the company's strategy to focus on controllable factors, highlighting progress on the Goodyear Forward program, which continues to deliver P&L benefits ahead of schedule.
  • Stewart indicated that broad-based price increases were implemented in the U.S. and Canada in response to tariffs and that Goodyear expanded product offerings in the premium segment, specifically noting growth in the greater than 18-inch market and the launch of new 18-inch plus SKUs.
  • Stewart stated, "we're paving the way for our organization to deliver increased value and focus on becoming #1 in tires and service," while also referencing a strong balance sheet supported by three divestitures committed in Goodyear Forward.
  • Christina L. Zamarro, CFO, shared, "about half of the miss in the quarter came in our Commercial business given materially weaker OEM replacement demand globally. The other half was driven by lower consumer OEM replacement volume."

Outlook

  • Zamarro outlined that Goodyear expects themes from the second quarter to persist in the near term, with commercial truck recovery not anticipated until 2026 and a forecasted decline in full year commercial earnings by $135 million compared to the prior forecast, with volumes expected to register below COVID year levels.
  • She indicated the near-term outlook for the Consumer business has weakened, with further global OE volume reductions and ongoing challenges in consumer replacement driven by U.S. market disruption and tariff risks in EMEA.
  • Zamarro said, "we expect that it will take longer for us to achieve our 2025 year-end margin and leverage objectives," although she reaffirmed confidence in exceeding original Goodyear Forward goals for cost savings and asset sale proceeds.
  • Third quarter volume is expected to be down about 5% globally, with higher unabsorbed fixed costs of $50 million and price/mix benefit of approximately $100 million.

Financial Results

  • Second quarter sales were $4.5 billion, down 2% from last year, with unit volume declining 5% due to global trade disruption and distributor buying patterns.
  • Gross margin declined 360 basis points; segment operating income was $159 million; Goodyear net income increased to $254 million, driven by a gain on the sale of the Dunlop brand.
  • After adjusting for significant items, the loss per share was ($0.17).
  • Free cash flow was stable versus last year, supported by $191 million in asset sale proceeds during the quarter. Net debt declined by over $600 million.
  • Americas unit volume decreased 2.6%, with commercial OE volume down 22% and nonmember imports growing 32%. EMEA reported a 2% unit volume decrease and a segment loss of $25 million. Asia Pacific saw a 16% unit volume decrease but maintained segment operating income at $43 million.

Q&A

  • Ryan Joseph Brinkman, JPMorgan: Questioned the impact and timing of low-cost import surges and tariff implementation. Christina L. Zamarro responded that "the imports that were coming into the U.S. still came in a big wave, and then we had a wave in Europe."
  • Brinkman also asked about price versus mix. Zamarro explained that recent price increases were effective, with commercial truck mix as a negative offset, and highlighted the launch of 11 new North America products focused on premium segments.
  • James Sylvester Mulholland, Deutsche Bank, inquired about the $74 million other cost headwind. Zamarro attributed this mainly to annualized inflation and increased tariff costs, expecting further increases in Q3 and Q4 as factory closures proceed.
  • Mulholland also asked about commercial headwinds. Zamarro indicated an additional $25 million in unabsorbed costs in the second half and new tariff costs due to higher rates in Brazil and Vietnam.
  • Thomas Jacob Scholl, BNP Paribas Exane, queried about mitigation efforts regarding rising tariffs and volume resets. Zamarro said supply chain adjustments and cost-saving actions are planned, but volume outlook for Q4 remains uncertain.
  • Scholl followed up on the ATD distributor exit and its impact on volumes. Stewart explained the strategy to work with aligned distributors and Zamarro noted that 95% of retailers transitioned by end of July.
  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe Research, asked about full-year SOI and free cash flow. Zamarro provided drivers for Q4 SOI, noting uncertainties around volume due to ongoing market churn and confirmed add-backs related to supply agreements not previously included.
  • Rosner also questioned longer-term confidence amid ongoing trade disruption. Zamarro and Stewart reiterated confidence in Goodyear’s U.S. manufacturing footprint and premium mix strategy, but timing of stabilization remains unclear.
  • Itay Michaeli, TD Cowen, asked about 2026 impacts and potential further cost cuts. Zamarro projected raw materials as a potential tailwind and Goodyear Forward as a benefit, but avoided specific guidance due to current uncertainty. Stewart confirmed no additional restructuring beyond current plans.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the impact of tariffs, volume uncertainty, and mitigation strategies, reflecting a slightly negative tone driven by skepticism about timing and effectiveness of management’s responses.
  • Management’s tone was generally defensive but confident, emphasizing cost control and strategic alignment. Stewart stated, "we are definitely positioned very well with our U.S. footprint," while Zamarro frequently noted uncertainties and the need to await stabilization.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone shifted from constructive to more pressing, while management moved from reporting confidence in momentum to a more cautious stance about near-term recovery timelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from clear targets for margin and leverage in Q1 to acknowledging delays in achieving 2025 objectives in Q2.
  • Strategic focus evolved from expansion and premium product launches to navigating significant trade disruptions and controlling costs.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on headwinds, mitigation actions, and timing, compared to Q1’s focus on growth opportunities and asset sales.
  • Commercial volume and earnings outlook deteriorated, with management now projecting commercial earnings to be $135 million lower than the prior forecast and volumes below COVID levels.
  • Management’s confidence in Goodyear Forward’s benefits persists, but with more emphasis on timing uncertainty and persistent headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Global trade disruption, rising tariffs, and continued surges of low-cost imports in the U.S. and Europe are key challenges.
  • Weakness in both consumer and commercial OE volumes, with commercial truck market performance expected below pandemic lows.
  • Additional risk from potential retroactive EU tariffs and ongoing distributor churn in the U.S. market.
  • Management is pursuing supply chain adjustments, cost-saving initiatives, and focus on premium product mix to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Goodyear management presented a cautious outlook for the remainder of 2025, citing persistent global trade disruptions, increased tariff impacts, and weaker than expected volumes across key segments. While the Goodyear Forward program continues to deliver on cost savings and asset sales, achieving previously set margin and leverage targets will take longer than anticipated. The company remains focused on premium product launches, strategic distributor partnerships, and aggressive cost controls, maintaining confidence in a return to earnings growth and stronger margins once market turbulence subsides.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NWPX Infrastructure anticipates Precast revenue strength and targets over $12M in Park-related bookings for 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NWPX Infrastructure, Inc. (NWPX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Scott J. Montross, CEO, highlighted “record-setting performance” for the quarter, noting that net sales reached $133.2 million, up 2.8% year-over-year, with strong operational execution across both business segments. He announced the company’s rebranding to NWPX Infrastructure, stating this “reflects our growth in the water infrastructure sector, removing geographic and product constraints while positioning us as a national solutions-driven infrastructure provider.”
  • The CEO reported that the Water Transmission Systems (WTS) segment, formerly Steel Pressure Pipe, delivered $84.6 million in revenue, down 5.5% year-over-year due to lower production volumes, though higher realized selling prices partly offset this. He noted “the impact of new trade policies that led to various customer-related shipping delays in the first quarter has largely subsided.” The WTS backlog improved over 20% to $348 million as of June 30 from $289 million as of March 31.
  • Precast segment revenue grew 21.5% year-over-year to a record $48.6 million, driven by sustained demand in the Geneva residential business. The CEO emphasized, “Gains were partially tempered by the slower to improve results in the nonresidential construction related portion of our Precast business, as broader macroeconomic headwinds, including effects from the new trade policies and persistently high interest rates continue to impact commercial construction activity.”
  • On strategic priorities, Montross said, “Our 2025 goal is to book $3 million of Park related products at Geneva… Our goal for 2025 remains to book over $12 million in Park related and other Precast projects outside of Texas with further benefits to come in 2026 and beyond.”
  • The CEO stated that the company is “currently prioritizing organic expansion due to lack of viable acquisition candidates,” but remains ready to move quickly on larger acquisition opportunities.
  • CFO Aaron Wilkins reported, “Through July 31, the final trading day in our most recent Rule 10b5-1 trading plan we repurchased approximately 363,000 shares or approximately $15 million worth of our common stock for an average price of $41.21 per share. This represents 3.6% of the ending shares outstanding at March 31, 2025.”

Outlook

  • The CEO anticipates WTS revenue and margins to “remain in line with or exceed those of the second quarter of 2025,” with modestly increasing production levels supporting improved overhead absorption. Full year 2025 bidding levels are projected to be “in line with or modestly higher than 2024.”
  • For Precast, the order book was $56 million as of June 30, with expectations for “Precast revenue to remain strong in the third quarter of 2025 with continued margin improvement versus the first 2 quarters of 2025.”
  • On a consolidated basis, management expects “revenues for the third quarter of 2025 to modestly improve from the third quarter of 2024.”
  • For the second half, “we continue to expect WTS revenues and margins to be similar to 2024 levels Precast revenue also being similar to 2024 levels, but with improved margins.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Wilkins reported consolidated net income of $9.1 million or $0.91 per diluted share, compared to $8.6 million or $0.86 per diluted share in the second quarter of 2024. Net sales increased to $133.2 million from $129.5 million in the prior year.
  • WTS segment sales decreased to $84.6 million from $89.5 million, due to a 10% reduction in tonnes produced, partially offset by a 4% increase in selling price per ton. Precast sales increased to $48.6 million from $40 million, driven by a 13% increase in volume shipped and a 7% increase in selling prices.
  • Consolidated gross profit was $25.4 million (19% margin), down 1.7% year-over-year. WTS gross profit was $15.1 million (17.8% margin). Precast gross profit was $10.3 million (21.2% margin).
  • SG&A expenses declined to $12.1 million, with an improved ratio of 9.1% of sales versus 9.4%. CapEx for the quarter was $3.5 million. Net cash provided by operating activities was $5.4 million. Positive free cash flow was $1.9 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Brent Edward Thielman, D.A. Davidson: Asked about the bifurcated Precast market trends and inflection points in nonresidential. CEO Montross responded that “order rate is picking up on the nonresidential side and gaining strength… revenue levels on the nonres are really starting to come up… pretty confident that nonres is going to be pretty strong through the rest of the year.”
  • Thielman asked if ramp-up in nonresidential would accelerate margins. Montross replied, “we expect the margins to improve in the second half, mainly due to the recovery and the growth of the nonresidential side business.”
  • Thielman inquired about WTS bidding for 2026. Montross shared, “you see a year in '26 that starts out like that with the potential of getting stronger later in the year.”
  • Julio Romero, Sidoti: Queried on trade/tariff impacts. Montross explained the trade policy effects have become “more of a nonevent” and described how tariffs at SLRC Mexico are now being passed on, with no major ongoing impact.
  • Romero further asked about customer behavior and new tariffs. Montross responded, “there was a little bit of confusion, which, quite frankly, there is always a little bit of confusion at the border with how things are being administered, and we had some details to work out… it really didn't have a major impact on us.”
  • Ted Jackson, Northland Securities: Asked about order intake-to-delivery timelines and the effect of new equipment. Montross detailed that Geneva is “burning through the order book that they're getting much quicker than they normally have because of the amount of orders.”
  • Jackson questioned if higher steel prices are driving backlog strength. Montross said the backlog increase is “more related to tons” than price, with steel prices “not really having a substantial impact.”
  • Jackson asked for the percentage of steel in costs. Wilkins responded, “it's still hovering about 30%.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were inquisitive but constructive, with a focus on segment performance, margin outlook, and tariff impacts. Tone was neutral to slightly positive, with persistent interest in margin trends and backlog sustainability.
  • Management’s tone was confident and matter-of-fact in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing adaptability and strong execution. Montross highlighted, “we are confident in our trajectory and our ability to scale our success.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a similar confidence level, though the Q2 tone reflected more optimism about backlog recovery and order flow.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted slightly from cautious optimism in Q1 (waiting for recovery in nonresidential and managing trade policy effects) to a more decisive outlook in Q2, with management anticipating improved Precast margins and a robust WTS backlog.
  • Q2 demonstrated a focus on record-setting Precast revenue and order book momentum, while Q1 was more impacted by trade policy disruptions and weather.
  • Analysts’ focus in Q2 shifted toward the pace of order fulfillment and the impact of operational efficiencies, compared to Q1 concerns about tariff-driven delays and shipment timing.
  • Management’s confidence in organic growth and cash flow generation increased quarter-over-quarter, especially for Precast and WTS segments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic headwinds, new trade policies, and high interest rates as ongoing challenges, particularly for nonresidential Precast.
  • Tariffs and administration at the border remain a logistical consideration, but management described these as manageable and not materially disruptive to current operations.
  • Analysts inquired about the impact of steel costs and tariff fluctuations, with management noting successful mitigation through pricing and flexible production strategies.

Final Takeaway

NWPX Infrastructure delivered record performance in Q2 2025, driven by strong Precast segment growth and a robust WTS order backlog. Management emphasized ongoing margin improvement, effective mitigation of trade policy impacts, and a continued focus on organic expansion and strategic capital allocation. The company expects continued revenue strength in both segments for the remainder of 2025, supported by a healthy order book, disciplined execution, and adaptability in shifting market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Heron Therapeutics raises adjusted EBITDA guidance to $9M-$13M for 2025 amid commercial restructuring and ZYNRELEF J-code milestone
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Heron Therapeutics (HRTX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Craig Alexander Collard highlighted the successful completion of new financing as “one of the most significant milestones this quarter,” stating it “strengthens our balance sheet, enhances our financial flexibility and positions us to accelerate our strategic initiatives with confidence.” Collard underscored strong operational and financial performance, with total net revenues of $37.2 million for the quarter, attributing the results to “disciplined execution and operational efficiency.” He also emphasized consistent product demand growth, especially for ZYNRELEF and APONVIE, outpacing net revenue growth over the past two quarters.
  • Collard announced the transition from a C-code to a permanent J-code for ZYNRELEF, effective October 1, as a move to “streamline reimbursement processes and reduce administrative burden,” which he believes will “improve the access and coverage across both government and commercial payers.”
  • COO Mark E. Hensley reported combined net revenues from APONVIE and ZYNRELEF of $10.7 million for the quarter, a year-over-year increase of 55.5%. He noted that “ZYNRELEF adoption continues to accelerate with growth in average daily units and total ordering accounts now more than 700 through the month of June.” Hensley detailed commercial changes including an enhanced per-unit compensation program with CrossLink and the creation of a dedicated APONVIE sales team, achieved “without incremental headcount costs.”
  • CFO Ira Duarte stated, “Our product gross profit for the 3 months ended June 30, 2025, was $27.3 million or 73.5%, which increased from 70.8% for the same period in 2024.” She also reported a net loss of $2.4 million for the quarter and cash and short-term investments of $40.6 million at the end of June.

Outlook

  • Collard confirmed maintenance of previously provided net revenue guidance of $153 million to $163 million for 2025. The company revised its adjusted EBITDA guidance from $4 million to $12 million to a new range of $9 million to $13 million, citing continued business performance. Management expects “wholesaler ordering patterns to normalize in Q3” following inventory drawdown for ZYNRELEF.
  • The transition to a permanent J-code for ZYNRELEF is anticipated to support broader product adoption in future periods, though management does not expect an immediate impact.

Financial Results

  • Heron reported total net revenues of $37.2 million for Q2 and $76.1 million for the first half of 2025. Product gross profit for Q2 was $27.3 million or 73.5%. SG&A expenses for Q2 were $26 million, attributed primarily to decreased personnel and related costs due to terminations and stock expense in 2024, partially offset by higher marketing costs for ZYNRELEF. Research and development expenses for Q2 were $2.9 million, down from $4.4 million in the prior year period.
  • The company reported a net loss of $2.4 million for Q2, with adjusted EBITDA for the quarter at a positive $1.8 million of operating income. Cash and short-term investments at quarter-end were $40.6 million.
  • On August 8, 2025, Heron entered a refinancing that includes a new $150 million credit facility, issuance of $35 million in convertible notes, a $28 million equity placement, and repayment or conversion of existing debt.

Q&A

  • Yuxi Dong, Jefferies: Asked for details on the VAN 400-milligram transition and its revenue impact for ZYNRELEF, and about the effect of the J-code and NOPAIN act on reimbursement and adoption. Mark E. Hensley responded that the inventory build-up “didn't normalize until Q2” and expects “Q3 normalization of inventory to be complete as of July 1.” Craig Alexander Collard noted the J-code is “much more sort of universally recognized” and, with NOPAIN, expects “a shift that where this J-code really does help us longer term.”
  • Brandon Richard Folkes, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about the ZYNRELEF sales force reorganization and the difference between APONVIE’s demand and revenue growth. Collard explained the team split allows for tailored expertise and that incentives with CrossLink have been enhanced. The revenue-demand gap is “primarily a wholesaler inventory adjustment” and “expected to normalize.”
  • Serge D. Belanger, Needham: Inquired whether the sales force changes represented a refocus or expansion, and about ZYNRELEF uptake since NOPAIN implementation. Hensley replied there is no expansion “beyond today over the short term” but more focus and some traditional hospital reps were added. Collard stated, “we haven't seen an immediate impact yet” from NOPAIN but expects “a real shift over the next year or so.”
  • Carl Edward Byrnes, Northland: Asked about the rate on the Hercules facility and net cash added post-closing. Ira Duarte answered the rate is “a little bit north of 10%” and net cash to the balance sheet is “about $11 million to $12 million after all expenses.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on inventory normalization, sales force strategy, and the effects of the J-code, suggesting a neutral-to-positive tone with persistent interest in operational execution and product uptake.
  • Management maintained a confident stance, emphasizing strategic alignment and future growth, with Collard stating, “we just feel like we have a lot of tailwinds that are pushing us in the right direction.” The sentiment in prepared remarks was confident, while Q&A responses were measured and occasionally cautious, especially regarding near-term impacts of reimbursement changes.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone remained steady, with continued focus on execution and growth. Management’s confidence level was consistent, though there was increased emphasis on transformative steps and financial discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a significant financing transaction and a revised adjusted EBITDA guidance, up from the prior range. Strategic focus shifted to commercial restructuring, with enhanced incentives and dedicated sales teams, while the permanent J-code for ZYNRELEF marked a new milestone for reimbursement.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA and gross profit margin remained strong, though Q2 net loss followed a profitable Q1. Revenue was relatively flat compared to Q1, attributed to inventory adjustments. Analysts’ focus on sales force changes and reimbursement differed from Q1’s emphasis on product adoption and litigation settlement outcomes.
  • Management’s tone in both quarters emphasized operational progress and disciplined execution, with the current quarter reflecting greater focus on future growth catalysts and financial flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged transient inventory drawdown impacting ZYNRELEF revenue, with expectations for normalization in Q3. There was also mention of increased marketing costs and continued competition in oncology.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing and impact of sales force changes and the actual uptake from reimbursement initiatives. Management’s mitigation includes targeted incentives, dedicated teams, and strategic use of new capital for expansion as needed.

Final Takeaway

Heron Therapeutics delivered a quarter marked by major financing, commercial realignment, and a permanent J-code milestone for ZYNRELEF, supporting long-term growth ambitions. Despite a temporary revenue impact from inventory normalization, management reaffirmed its full-year revenue target and raised adjusted EBITDA guidance, citing operational discipline and a focused go-to-market strategy. The company enters the second half of 2025 well positioned for accelerating adoption of its core products, underpinned by enhanced reimbursement and sales execution initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Iovance falls after missing Q2 expectation
data.description:
Iovance Biotherapeutics (NASDAQ:IOVA) shares tanked after the company failed to meet top-line as well as bottom-line expectations. 

The company reported a quarterly revenue of $60M, missing estimate by $7.14M, hurt by sales of Proleukin. It also posted a loss per share of $0.33, falling short of analysts expectation by $0.04. 

Iovance reiterated full year total revenue outlook in the range between $250M and $300M.

"Growth for Amtagvi and Proleukin will continue in the second half of 2025 as existing ATC growth continues and large community practices begin treating patients," Frederick Vogt, CEO of Iovance stated.

The stock is down 23%.

data.title: SANUWAVE outlines Q3 revenue target of $12M–$12.7M as new applicator production nears launch
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SANUWAVE Health, Inc. (SNWV) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Morgan C. Frank opened by noting, “Q2 was another strong performance for SANUWAVE, up 42% year-on-year on the top line and bringing us to 51% year-on-year for the first 6 months of 2025.” He emphasized system sales growth, stating, “We sold 116 UltraMIST systems in Q2, a 61% increase versus a year ago and an 18% increase in Q1.” Frank highlighted commercial momentum, a fully staffed national sales team, and new commercial operations leadership, adding, “As of mid-July, for the first time in my tenure as CEO, we have all 12 of our national sales territory staffed and have added a full-time national and key accounts manager to focus on our big accounts.”

  • Frank described the internal environment as “the team has more than risen to the challenge,” and set the stage for the next quarter by saying, “If Q1 and Q2 were sort of max disruption, Q3 is really shaping up to be the quarter of maximum construction.” He previewed SANUWAVE’s first-ever outbound marketing campaign slated for October and noted increasing inbound inquiries and market adoption.

  • CFO Peter Sorensen stated, “The second quarter was a strong one for SANUWAVE, with revenue reaching a new Q2 record and growing 42% year-over-year. This performance reflects continued momentum in our commercial strategy and growing demand for UltraMIST.” Sorensen also cited gross margin improvement, “Gross margin as a percentage of revenue amounted to 78.3% for the 3 months ended June 30, 2025, versus 73.2% for the same period last year.”

Outlook

  • Frank provided guidance for the upcoming quarter: “we're guiding to $12 million to $12.7 million in Q3 revenues.” He cautioned that “Q3 is a tough comp for us as it's up against the pigs or python quarter last year in which we had one really large sale sort of tipped the scales and drive a surge to an 89% year-on-year growth rate.” Annual guidance remains unchanged.

  • Frank indicated the company is “not including any of that [large, unpredictable customer deals] in the guidance above,” stressing a conservative approach while learning how to forecast large customer ramp-ups.

Financial Results

  • Sorensen reported, “Revenue for the 3 months ended June 30, 2025, totaled $10.2 million, an increase of 42% as compared to $7.2 million for the same period of 2024.” Gross margin was “78.3% for the 3 months ended June 30, 2025, versus 73.2% for the same period last year.”

  • Operating income “totaled $1.9 million, which is slightly down by the $0.1 million compared to the same period last year.” Operating expenses were $6.1 million, up $2.9 million year-over-year, primarily due to higher non-cash stock-based compensation and the absence of a prior-period accrual release. Net income was $1.1 million, down from $6.6 million last year, which had been boosted by a one-time noncash gain.

  • EBITDA for the quarter was $3.2 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $3.4 million, a $1.9 million year-over-year improvement. Cash as of June 30, 2025, totaled $8.5 million.

Q&A

  • Kyle Royal Bauser, ROTH Capital Partners: Questioned gross margin impact of the new applicator design and operating leverage. Frank responded, “We suspect…we can pick up probably 350, 400 basis points of additional margin on the applicators with the new design.” Sorensen added this improvement would be gradual and visible in early 2026 as inventory cycles through.

  • Bauser asked about sales force expansion. Frank replied, “at this point, we're pretty much where we need to be to have real national coverage,” but did not rule out adding a few more reps later in the year.

  • On marketing focus, Bauser inquired about target segments. Frank described a tailored approach, specifying, “a lot of it is going to be starting to really both differentiate and target the offering, so that we can engage with mobile wound care, with nursing homes, with skilled nursing facilities… we're starting to engage there with a bit more focus.”

  • Bauser asked about senior secured debt. Frank provided an update: “We received several term sheets that we thought were very attractive. And we chose one and are currently in the process of working to close it...a significant improvement over what we have.”

  • Christopher Michael Plahm, Tall Pines Capital, asked about large customer pipeline. Frank highlighted, “we were recently added to the approved vendor list on one of the largest sort of hospital chains and networks in the U.S. And so if that's become a very…interesting hunting ground for us,” noting the newly hired key accounts rep.

  • Albert Hanser, Kestrel, asked about intellectual property. Frank said, “SANUWAVE is sitting on a large patent portfolio that includes a lot of things to do with shockwave and with ultrasound…we have in the past monetized some of these patents,” and referenced ongoing licensing interest.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on gross margin expansion, sales force scale, marketing, debt refinancing, and large customer opportunities, with a generally positive but probing tone for more detail on execution and financial impact.

  • Management’s tone was confident and optimistic in prepared remarks, using phrases like “the team has more than risen to the challenge” and “we now have a team that's really committed to getting SANUWAVE firing on all cylinders,” but slightly more cautious and measured in the Q&A, especially regarding timing for margin improvements and large client onboarding.

  • Compared to Q1, management’s tone shifted from highlighting disruption and restructuring to emphasizing stability, growth, and operational readiness, while analysts’ questions remained focused on execution and future scalability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 revenue is $12 million to $12.7 million, up from Q2’s $10 million to $10.7 million guidance in Q1.

  • Management is now focused on "maximum construction" after two quarters of "max disruption," signaling a transition from restructuring to growth.

  • The sales force is now fully staffed with all 12 territories covered, compared to ongoing build-out and sales leadership changes in Q1.

  • Gross margin remains high but slightly down from Q1, attributed to engineering costs; however, future margin improvement is anticipated with the new applicator design.

  • Analysts continue to probe debt refinancing, large account momentum, and marketing plans, but are now also asking about operational leverage and scalability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the challenge of forecasting large customer deals, with Frank stating, “we're really still in the learning phase on how to time forecasting with these bigger customers and how they ramp up.”

  • Gross margin pressure from upfront engineering costs was cited, with mitigation expected from new production processes in 2026.

  • Debt refinancing remains an area of focus, but management expressed confidence in improvement, emphasizing attractive new terms under negotiation.

  • Customer concentration risk appears reduced, as “only 1 customer exceeding 5% of revenues.”

Final Takeaway

SANUWAVE Health reported continued top-line growth and record Q2 revenues, supported by robust UltraMIST system sales and strong gross margins. With a fully staffed national sales team, new commercial leadership, and the upcoming launch of a more efficient applicator design, the company targets $12 million to $12.7 million in Q3 revenue. Management highlighted a shift from restructuring to operational build-out, a conservative approach to forecasting large deals, and active debt refinancing efforts, positioning the company for further growth and margin expansion in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DiamondRock Hospitality maintains 2025 RevPAR growth range of -1% to +1% with strong out-of-room spend trends
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey John Donnelly opened by emphasizing the company's focus on driving outsized free cash flow per share growth and highlighted strategies such as recycling out of low free cash flow yield hotels, reinvesting in higher-yielding assets, and continuing share repurchases when valuation disconnects exist. Donnelly stated, “To achieve this end, the following is what you should expect from DiamondRock, recycling out of low free cash flow yield hotels into higher-yielding investments, capitalizing on opportunities to dispose of assets where buyers see greater value than we do, reinvesting in our assets when and where outsized ROIs exist, not just outsized RevPAR growth, thoughtfully stretching the renovation life cycle... and reinvesting in ourselves through share repurchases when a valuation disconnect exists.”
  • The company completed a $1.5 billion refinancing and extension of its senior unsecured credit facility, increasing its size from $1.2 billion and maintaining the pricing grid. As a result, all debt will be prepayable at any time without cost or penalty, and there will be no debt maturities until 2029.
  • DRH repurchased just under 1.7 million common shares at an average price of $7.46 during the quarter, with 3.6 million shares repurchased year-to-date for $27.3 million. $146.8 million of capacity remains on the repurchase authorization.
  • The Cliffs at L'Auberge project in Sedona is expected to drive a 25 to 50 basis point tailwind to RevPAR growth in 2026, with wedding revenues at the Cliffs projected to more than double over the full year 2024. Donnelly shared, “Despite the elevated revenue disruption from waiting for a certificate of occupancy, transient and group bookings are now accelerating. Wedding revenues at the Cliffs in this partial year are expected to more than double the full year of 2024.”
  • CFO Briony R. Quinn noted, “Comparable RevPAR growth in the second quarter was 0.1%, driven by a 1.1% increase in rate and an 80 basis point decline in occupancy. RevPAR was negatively impacted by approximately 50 basis points due to our ongoing conversion of the Orchards Inn in Sedona to the Cliffs at L'Auberge.” She also highlighted, “Corporate adjusted EBITDA was $90.5 million and adjusted FFO per share was $0.35. Finally, free cash flow per share for the trailing 12 months... increased approximately 4.5% to $0.63 per share.”

Outlook

  • The company is maintaining its full year outlook for RevPAR growth of negative 1% to plus 1%. Donnelly stated, “We are maintaining our full year outlook for RevPAR growth of negative 1% to plus 1%, but we are encouraged by the increased out-of-room spending trends we experienced in Q2 and early Q3.”
  • Total RevPAR growth is now expected to outperform RevPAR growth by 50 basis points in 2025, an increase from the prior assumption of in-line performance.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2025 is $275 million to $295 million, up $2.5 million at the midpoint from prior guidance. FFO per share guidance for 2025 is $0.96 to $1.06, up $0.01 at the midpoint.
  • Projected capital expenditures remain unchanged at $85 million to $95 million.
  • For Q3, DRH expects RevPAR to be down in the low single digits, with the toughest comparisons in August.

Financial Results

  • Comparable RevPAR growth in Q2 2025 was 0.1%. Total RevPAR growth was 1.1% due to a 4.2% increase in out-of-room revenues per occupied room, which reached a quarterly high of $160 per occupied room.
  • Food and beverage revenues increased 3.1%, with F&B profit growing over 6% and margins rising 105 basis points.
  • Operating expenses increased 0.7% (excluding a property tax increase in Chicago), while full portfolio expense growth was 2.6%. Hotel EBITDA margins contracted 97 basis points, but would have increased 30 basis points excluding the Chicago tax increase.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA was $90.5 million and adjusted FFO per share was $0.35. Free cash flow per share for the trailing 12 months increased 4.5% to $0.63.
  • Urban portfolio achieved 3% RevPAR growth, while resort portfolio comparable RevPAR declined 6.3%. The delayed opening of the Cliffs at L'Auberge impacted resort results.

Q&A

  • Smedes Rose, Citi, questioned the stabilization at the higher end of the portfolio. CEO Donnelly clarified the comment referred to the overall portfolio, stating, “we are moving towards stabilization. So bottom line, it was meant to be a comment speaking about fundamentals improving from sort of a softer point in time.”
  • Rose also asked about Q3 RevPAR declines—COO Justin L. Leonard attributed this to difficult comps, particularly due to the Democratic National Convention in Chicago last year, not a change in trend.
  • Cooper R. Clark, Wells Fargo, inquired about share buybacks and the preferred stock. CEO Donnelly responded that buybacks remain attractive and the preferred redemption is not in 2025 guidance but will be considered as the year progresses.
  • Clark asked about Sedona's repositioning; Donnelly said forward bookings for the Cliffs show encouraging group business and rate increases of $150 to $200 year-over-year.
  • Austin Todd Wurschmidt, KeyBanc, sought detail on group pace improvements. CEO Donnelly said most improvement is in urban hotels, with Q4 group optimism due to more available booking weeks. Boston is expected to be the strongest group market in 2026.
  • Chris Jon Woronka, Deutsche Bank, raised the issue of potential competition from cruise lines in resort markets. Donnelly said it is considered, but difficult to isolate its impact.
  • Duane Thomas Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI, asked about industry RevPAR and refinancing impact. Donnelly expressed hope for RevPAR acceleration in 2026, while CFO Quinn highlighted increased flexibility from the recent refinancing.
  • Daniel Patrick Hogan, Baird, questioned sustainability of out-of-room spend growth. Donnelly said it's too early to predict for 2026 and believes price is a bigger driver than volume.
  • Chris Darling, Green Street, asked about asset sales and portfolio size. Donnelly said asset sales will be balanced with capital recycling and that being a smaller REIT is not a major concern.
  • Floris Van Dijkum, Ladenburg, discussed valuation metrics. Donnelly advocated for incorporating after CapEx metrics like free cash flow per share into valuation.
  • Kenneth G. Billingsley, Compass Point, asked about group business and competition. Management said large hotels rely on brand channels while independents focus on smaller, high-dollar groups. Billingsley also asked about disposition hurdles, to which Donnelly cited tax and credit market volatility, but said no current obstacles remain.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about Q3 RevPAR declines, group booking pace, and asset disposition timing, with a tone that was generally neutral to slightly negative.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone, with Donnelly stating, “our operating results and forward bookings indicate we are possibly entering a more stable operating environment than we were experiencing just 3 months ago.” Management was more upbeat in prepared remarks and cautious but reassured during Q&A.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sounded more constructive about stabilization but remained mindful of macro risks and comps.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The full year RevPAR growth guidance remained unchanged at -1% to +1%, but management now expects total RevPAR growth to outperform RevPAR growth by 50 basis points in 2025, an upgrade from the prior in-line assumption.
  • Corporate adjusted EBITDA guidance midpoint increased by $2.5 million and FFO per share midpoint by $0.01 versus Q1.
  • The Q2 call highlighted increased out-of-room spending, improved share repurchase activity, and successful refinancing, while the Q1 call focused more on cost controls, group booking uncertainties, and the impact of calendar shifts.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from renovation costs and group conversion pauses in Q1 to Q3 comps and asset disposition timing in Q2. Management’s tone was incrementally more confident about stabilization and future growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macro uncertainty, challenging comps from one-time events in 2024, and delays in asset dispositions due to regulatory and credit market volatility.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of out-of-room spend growth and the competitive landscape for group business.
  • Mitigation strategies include cost controls, share repurchases, maintaining balance sheet flexibility, and focusing on high-ROI renovations and asset recycling.

Final Takeaway

DiamondRock Hospitality’s Q2 2025 call reinforced its focus on disciplined capital allocation, with continued share repurchases, a strengthened balance sheet, and targeted asset renovations expected to drive free cash flow per share growth. Management maintained its RevPAR growth outlook and signaled confidence in stabilization trends, particularly in group demand and out-of-room revenue, while remaining attentive to macroeconomic headwinds and competitive pressures in both urban and resort portfolios.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
